stem: The matrix \( B \) is similar to \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \); that is, \( B = P^{-1} A P \) for some invertible \( P \). Similar matrices have the same determinant. What is \( \det(B) \)?
solution: Similar matrices share the same determinant, so \( \det(B) = \det(A) = ([[a]])([[d]]) - ([[b]])([[c]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the products instead of subtracting; the determinant is \( ad - bc \).
This is the negative of the determinant.
This is the trace \( a+d \), another invariant, but not the determinant.
This multiplies the wrong pairs of entries.
This is one more than the correct determinant.
This is two less than the correct determinant.

stem: Suppose \( B = P^{-1} A P \) for some invertible \( P \), where \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \). Similar matrices have the same trace. What is \( \operatorname{tr}(B) \)?
solution: Similar matrices share the same trace, so \( \operatorname{tr}(B) = \operatorname{tr}(A) = [[a]] + [[d]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the determinant, another shared invariant, but not the trace.
This subtracts the diagonal entries instead of adding them.
This multiplies the diagonal entries instead of adding them.
This sums the off-diagonal entries, which is not the trace.
This is one more than the correct trace.
This is three less than the correct trace.

stem: Suppose \( B \) is similar to \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[d1]] & [[u12]] & [[u13]] \\ 0 & [[d2]] & [[u23]] \\ 0 & 0 & [[d3]] \end{bmatrix} \). Similar matrices have exactly the same eigenvalues (with the same multiplicities). What is the largest eigenvalue of \( B \)?
solution: \( A \) is upper triangular, so its eigenvalues are exactly its diagonal entries \( [[d1]], [[d2]], [[d3]] \). Since \( B \) is similar to \( A \), it has the very same eigenvalues; the largest is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is one of the diagonal entries of \( A \), but not necessarily the largest.
This is one of the diagonal entries of \( A \), but not necessarily the largest.
This is one of the diagonal entries of \( A \), but not necessarily the largest.
This is the trace (sum of all eigenvalues), not the largest eigenvalue alone.
This is one more than the correct largest eigenvalue.
This is two less than the correct largest eigenvalue.

stem: Suppose \( B \) is similar to \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ [[d]] & [[e]] & [[f]] \\ [[g]] & [[h]] & [[i]] \end{bmatrix} \) (note the third row is a combination of the first two). Similar matrices have the same rank. What is \( \operatorname{rank}(B) \)?
solution: Similar matrices share the same rank, so \( \operatorname{rank}(B) = \operatorname{rank}(A) \). Since the third row of \( A \) is a linear combination of the first two, \( A \) cannot have full rank; computing directly gives \( \operatorname{rank}(A) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: The third row is a combination of the first two, so \( A \) cannot have full rank \( 3 \).
\( A \) is built from rows that are generically nonzero, so it is not the zero matrix and cannot have rank \( 0 \) in general.
This overshoots the true rank by one.
This adds up unrelated matrix entries instead of computing a rank.
This computes a single 2x2 minor rather than the rank of the full matrix.
This mixes a row difference with the combination coefficient \( k \), not a rank computation.
This undershoots the true rank by one.

stem: Suppose \( B = P^{-1} A P \) for some invertible \( P \), where \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \). Note that \( B^{[[n]]} = P^{-1} A^{[[n]]} P \) is then similar to \( A^{[[n]]} \). What is \( \det\!\left(B^{[[n]]}\right) \)?
solution: Since \( B^{[[n]]} \) is similar to \( A^{[[n]]} \), \( \det\!\left(B^{[[n]]}\right) = \det\!\left(A^{[[n]]}\right) = \det(A)^{[[n]]} = \left([[a]]\cdot[[d]] - [[b]]\cdot[[c]]\right)^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies \( \det(A) \) by \( n \) instead of raising it to the \( n \)-th power.
This forgets to raise \( \det(A) \) to the power \( n \) at all.
This raises the trace, not the determinant, to the \( n \)-th power.
This uses the negative of \( \det(A) \) before raising to the power \( n \).
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.
